tier1.title: Conceptual Foundation
tier1.content: Momentum describes 'quantity of motion': p = mv. In isolated systems, total momentum is conserved. Is this a collision, explosion, or interaction over time? Are external forces negligible during the short interaction?

tier2.title: Relevant Principles
tier2.content: Momentum: p = mv. Impulse: J = FőĒt = őĒp. Conservation: p_total,before = p_total,after (if ő£F_ext ‚Čą 0). Elastic collision: KE also conserved. Inelastic: objects may stick together; KE not conserved. Explosions: internal forces push pieces apart.

tier3.title: Targeted Guidance
tier3.content: Define 'before' and 'after' clearly. For 1D collisions, use signed velocities. For 2D, conserve momentum separately in x and y. If objects stick (perfectly inelastic), they share final velocity: m‚āĀv‚āĀ + m‚āāv‚āā = (m‚āĀ + m‚āā)v_f.

tier4.title: Near-Solution Scaffold
tier4.content: Write momentum conservation: m‚āĀv‚āĀŠĶĘ + m‚āāv‚āāŠĶĘ = m‚āĀv‚āĀf + m‚āāv‚āāf. If elastic in 1D, add KE conservation: ¬Ĺm‚āĀv‚āĀŠĶĘ¬≤ + ¬Ĺm‚āāv‚āāŠĶĘ¬≤ = ¬Ĺm‚āĀv‚āĀf¬≤ + ¬Ĺm‚āāv‚āāf¬≤. Solve the system. For impulse problems: FőĒt = mőĒv.

tier1.title: Conceptual Foundation
tier1.content: Impulse changes momentum. A large force over a short time can produce the same őĒp as a smaller force over longer time. What changes momentum in this problem ‚ÄĒ a collision or a sustained force?

tier2.title: Relevant Principles
tier2.content: Impulse-momentum theorem: J = őĒp = m(v_f ‚ąí v_i). Average force: F_avg = őĒp/őĒt. In recoil problems (gun/bullet, person on skates), total momentum starts at zero. Center of mass velocity is unchanged by internal forces.

tier3.title: Targeted Guidance
tier3.content: Choose a sign convention before substituting numbers. For explosions at rest: 0 = m‚āĀv‚āĀ + m‚āāv‚āā. For hitting a wall and bouncing, őĒp = m(v_f ‚ąí v_i) ‚ÄĒ note that reversing direction means v_f and v_i have opposite signs.

tier4.title: Near-Solution Scaffold
tier4.content: Calculate initial total momentum. Set equal to final total momentum. If one unknown, solve directly. For two unknowns with elastic collision, use both momentum and KE equations. Check: does your answer make physical sense (e.g., lighter object moves faster after explosion)?
